sentences.text: All right.

I was just telling you that my father scolded me, and he told me that if I succeed and the henchmen-- his henchmen-- and I would kill Hitler, and his henchmen would continue, it could be worse than before.
And the Germans, who were still euphoric because they, at that time, they were victorious everywhere.
And you could read [INAUDIBLE], wherever you cared to look, [SPEAKING GERMAN], Germany seeks on all frontiers.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] as I say, my father was against it.
He said in the game of-- life is like a game of chess.
You should have thought about the consequences.
And I had-- all I was thinking about was that if Hitler is going to die, if I should be successful, then Mother and Sister would hopefully come back.
Of course, they didn't live anymore, but we didn't know that.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the first thing we have to do is now prepare to flee.
And we shall do that in the morning.
I should be able to gather some food for us.
I had some contact with the kitchen.
And we shall be prepared.
I said, maybe we should flee now.
He said, no, hang on.
Now it was another hour or so I slept, and I saw engines.
An engine in my dream, I saw shooting up high in the air where I blocked it, and immediately everybody stared at me, and I found myself in the fields and guard dogs after me.
And I woke up in a sweat, and it was still too early for the workers to get up.
And I got up.
And again, I stole myself out of the camp towards the station.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I couldn't go very near.
I saw a train there, a short train.
I couldn't go very near.
In the middle of the train, I saw a bare-headed man, and the hairstyle and mustache was unmistakably Hitler.
I was going to go a little nearer, but there were soldiers there.
And I didn't dare.
And the train had already started rolling, and as it is in woods, I saw the picture here.
Now you see it, now you don't, now you see it, now you don't.
Now I was standing now, and waiting, mesmerized, to hear and listen for the crash that might have changed or would change history.
As I said it before, might have changed.
Because I'll never know what happened, but the train went on.
There was no crash.
Now, whether some patrol found the obstacle or a local farmer saw it and removed it out of fear, I don't know.
And I was at first very disappointed.
Only later, when I thought about this incident, and thinking what happened when, as in the beginning of my story, one German was shot in Paris, and they burned down all the synagogues.
Under the pretext what I had done, the Holocaust would then be put-- the finger would have pointed at me.
And those moments are only consolation, because it was autumn, 1940-42.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Although, perhaps, I would guess 2 million Jews have been killed at that time already, but the bulk was still to come.
The worst was still to come.
And I said, if his henchmen had continued with the Holocaust, they would have had this excuse, I'm sure.
Now, something to go back a little.
Just before my mother left, there was a conversation going on.
And in this conversation, a member of the family [? or two ?] said, it's rumored that 2 million Jews have already been killed.
What's going to happen to us?
And my mother said, what's going to happen to all Jews is going to happen to us.
And something stirred in me.
And I said to myself, in my youthful bravura, if there's one Jew to survive, it will be me.
And at that time, I was-- believed in it.
Later, of course, I thought, how many others thought the same way?
And it really, as future events show, you could have been the strongest, the healthiest, the cleverest.
If you didn't have the element of luck, luck, you wouldn't have survived.
It's the game of the dice.
Anyway, this was my little episode of the two episodes in the [INAUDIBLE] camp of Klaj.
A month or two later, unceremoniously, we were packed into a train and off we had to go.
Never, they never gave us a reason.
And--
Yes, yeah.
Get into a train.
Yes, well, we had-- yes, we had a suitcase.
One thing I would like to-- I would like to sort of mention here that while we were back at the house, my father and I, he put his photograph into a jacket which he unfortunately forgot in the house.
And we never returned there.
So one of the photographs was-- was lost already.
Now, when we were on the train, there were only 150 people.
So we were not--
No, two or three carriages.
It wasn't sort of cramped.
We didn't go very far, either.
Although we have learned-- we had learned already that-- we've heard, of course, about the SS and the camps.
But we didn't know much about it.
And we noticed that we were not going east.
So in one way, we were glad about it, because east was unknown.
You never heard anything about it.
On the other hand, we were thinking, maybe if we did go east, we'd join Mother and Sister.
So we're in two minds whether to be glad or not glad.
Anyway, before long the journey was finished, and we ended in the famous-- or the infamous-- camp of Krakau-Plaszow.
Forced labor camp Krakau-Plaszow.
[GERMAN], Krakau-Plaszow.
And the very first day, we learned that the commandant of this camp, which is the camp where Schindler got his people from, boasted that he had killed 80,000 people with his own hand.
Boasted that he was the biggest murderer of all time and he had killed 80,000 people with his own hand.
Something to look forward to.
Now, we went into that camp, we had to stand in line.
Three-- what I believe were Ukrainians-- were walking up and down in front of us.
They didn't like-- they had each one with a rifle, but it didn't look like shooting us.
They wouldn't have brought us there to shoot us.
Well, maybe.
I will say all sort of thoughts went through my mind, but why are we waiting?
And amongst the few valuables we still had, I had a pocket watch.
Neither silver nor gold, but a watch at that time was already something enormous.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I took it on its chain, and I sort of swung it, and as the-- one of the Ukrainians came near, I said, what are you waiting for?
And he said, delousing.
Not ready yet.
And before he said it, he-- he took a-- took a split second-- the watch was in his pocket already.
And he walked on.
And I looked at my father, if he would be angry with me, but he wasn't.
But it would have been that much less in case we really-- really, at a later stage, would need it for bribes, food, who knows what.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Anyway, delousing took place, that means some-- some kind of a bath.
And our suits were put into a process of whatever it was for delousing.
When I came out, our other suitcases were still there.
And I just-- just thought, well, German efficiency is not as good as it used to be.
Because our other suitcase where we had more suits were not taken away.
But the next step annoyed me very much.
Some-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] people came, they were obviously prisoners, and it took a brush.
And on our suits they painted a stripe on one side, a stripe on another, and a few across, back and front.
And for good measure, to look like a clown, that was truly unnecessary, put a red dot on the knees.
Well, I was angry, but then I still had to laugh.
Because as I say, if I really want to escape, I've got another suit there.
This was a-- a camp where we still had our things.
Later, that we will--
Yeah.
No, well, no.
Of course, we had the suit on-- on which they made these stripes.
Of course, it's for just a further step in degrading and humiliating us, I'm not sure.
But anyway, it would be more difficult to-- to run away with a suit like this.
And that's why I thought, I have another one.
You know.
Anyway, we got barracks.
First there were like long boards and three tiers, and we slept at the top tier.
And we were working in the streets.
And I found in later camps, wherever you went, newcomers always got the worst.
And at this particular stretch, it was good old Amon Goeth, we called him 'Get,' a commandant of Plaszow, who-- his villa wasn't far away.
And when he went-- when he got up-- I'm not sure whether that was before breakfast or after, but it was some kind of a morning exercise, he looked through the-- through his telescope.
He had a rifle with a telescope.
If somebody idled or rested a minute on his pick axe or shovel, he would be shot.
That would soon make sure that all the others worked until they would drop.
At that time, I developed something which I already discussed with my father, all systems go for survival.
Don't be conspicuous.
And whatever you do, everything has to be sort of worked out.
And to work that way and the food we got, I'm sure we wouldn't have lasted long.
You would have to take a rest, and that was dangerous.
So what I did, I put the shovel into the ground, but I expected that somebody would see me moving.
And that's the important thing, don't stand still.
And took the almost empty shovel out and with the movement as if I had, you know, done the work.
And I worked with my eyes.
I looked and all around, and looked.
You know, if somebody would come near who looked suspicious to me, as either a guard or one of the Germans, of course I could work harder.
And that was sort of my first invention amongst, later, others who just thought it has to be geared to survive.
And we then-- we looked around if we find somebody who we knew.
And in this particular camp, there was Jewish police like in the ghettos.
They-- they then were transported to the camps.
The Jewish police were dressed almost like the Polish police, and they had even ranks amongst them.
Ordinary, one stripe, two stripes, and even the highest one is three stripes.
His name was Hilowic, but he plays a role a bit later.
My father, amongst the police, found a cousin.
Now, via this cousin, we got a little bit of food and a different job in the metal works.
I think when we have a break now, I'm going to prepare a book to show you these things in the book.
Because as we are speaking, we could have shown you that in a film where it was, because I have a sketch map of this particular camp.
Now, also at one time, I was introduced to a police with two stripes.
His name was Salz.
And he gave me some food as well, being in, you know, in the kitchen.
He also would play another role later in my story.
Now, it was a mighty change from working hard with little food to working not so hard in the metal works, metal workshop, with more food.
Although it didn't come regular, but now and again.
Now, the work in a metal workshop was that outside this, these barracks, was a very, very large heap of discarded metal of all sorts.
I only found out much after the war that it probably was from the famous Schindler's [?
Emalia ?] works where a lot of metal came as well.
At that time, I didn't know.
Had I known about Schindler in those days, I would done anything and everything to get into his group.
Now, we-- or rather, I-- had to gather pieces which were suitable for the machinery we had inside to stamp out, of all things, shovels for sweeping.
My father had a job to clean them up and paint them.
Now, at one stage, was-- I was just on top of the heap sorting out some metal at the bottom, [? being ?] down to the bottom.
And I saw two others also engaged in that particular-- they had other jobs, whatever they manufactured, hinges and other things.
And I saw them talking to each other.
And I said, went and climbed down and I wanted to tell them, don't do that.
That's the most dangerous thing that you can do, talk to each other.
You-- you do that inside, not-- not here, outside.
But having the intention to tell them not to talk, I soon stood there and listened to the story of the younger one.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was only there for two days.
He was about 16.
His parents and the family, they were hidden with Polish people, and they were kept as long as the money, jewelry, and goodies lasted.
And then they were-- had to leave.
And they were in the woods and getting hungry, so he, at one time, went to a farmer where he was successfully begging, and he got his sausage.
His father, he knew his father wouldn't eat it, not yet, because it wasn't kosher.
But he would look away if his family did.
And eggs and other things.
He came back full of joy to the woods to find [? this-- ?] a couple of empty suitcases and some of their things strewn all over the place.
Of course, had a big shock.
What should he do?
He went in a nearby village and he, come what may, I have to find my people.
He asked the Polish police, they couldn't tell him.
They sent him to the Germans.
The Germans didn't tell him anything, either, but put him to the camp.
And at this part of the story, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he took out a small packet of photographs and was showing it to us, was going to show it to us.
And as he-- as another, the other fellow, quite a tall fellow, bent down to look at it, he got the bullet right here.
And standing on the side, he fell forward.
And I was trying to-- towards me, I was trying to hold him.
And his blood splashed all over my hands, but I couldn't hold him.
He fell across my feet.
The second bullet tore the collar of my jacket.
Immediately, in shock, I put my hand against that.
It's an automatic reaction.
My knees buckled.
I don't know whether I was clever at that moment, but I couldn't think, it was too quick.
But I let myself drop.
This third shop-- shot felled the youngster, and he fell across my arm.
And I was sort of lying face down, almost right to the ground, and I had my mouth open and I was listening.
I heard steps coming towards me, and now I thought, it's the end.
But if the end is in front of someone, supposedly his life goes past the person in front of him, in front of his eyes, they're thinking about it.
But what I could think of was a contest at school, six boys contesting who could keep one's breath longest.
I won, and all I could think, hold your breath.
Do not breathe, do not breathe.
You must.
You won then, you must win now.
You must win now.
Anyway, the steps moved away.
Apparently I had more blood on me than almost the other two, from both of them, you know.
So he was happy that he had killed with only three shots, three people.
A nice little story for his party tonight.
Now, I take up the story as now--
That's-- I take up the story as my father tells it for a little while.
My father was working with me inside.
And when the first shot-- when he heard the first shot, he turned around and just heard the-- when he looked through the window, he heard a second shot and saw me drop.
He wanted to go outside, you know, and he was held back by the others.
Don't go, you will kill us all.
You're too late.
You'll kill us all.
He had to listen, and they held him anyway.
So what should he do?
He told me that after a while, people looked out and saw to say, called out when the coast was clear.
Not sure if they-- what's the equivalent in German.
Anyway, they were all gone.
[SPEAKING GERMAN] ?]
And so my father could not, at this moment, be held back anymore.
And he went outside, didn't go directly to us.
Sort of pretended to bend down and pick up some of the metal.
And as he came near, he saw me.
But I didn't know who it was.
He was sobbing.
I still didn't know.
And he started to say the Kaddish.
The usual way of saying Kaddish is with-- with 10 people and a minyan.
But when I heard his voice, I said in German, Papa, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
Daddy, Daddy, I'm not hurt.
So his prayers stopped and, as he told me, so did nearly his heart.
And then I immediately sort of told him, don't come near, watch the towers and see what you can do.
So he went inside and, of course, told them the amazing story.
And I didn't know, should I get up, should I not?
What is the procedure?
Will they collect us?
I can't-- I can't allow them to bury me.
I'm sort of not quite there yet.
On the other hand, getting up and walking away, although the watchtower people were used to these scenes, I'm sure, but for one of the dead to get up and wander off must be a bit rarer.
That's almost a bit of humor there.
And so as I was thinking, a number of people came out.
But they said, don't.
We are just finding out what we should do.
Don't.
We don't collect you yet, hang on a bit.
After a little while longer, six came out, took us, one each on their shoulders, and walked us around a corner, out of sight of the watchtowers.
There they put us down.
Of course, two stayed down, and me, sort of rubbing my limbs, get the circulation going.
And I got up and marched behind them into the workshops.
There I was looking for brown or red paint, because I was full of blood.
And paint, just where we run out of this particular color.
And I didn't know what to do, nobody knew the procedure.
Would Goeth send somebody to collect three people, or what's going to be?
If they find only two, well, you know, nobody knew.
It was-- you know, we were working in two shifts of 12.
Evening shift finished, went away from the workshop into our barracks, past the two people whose name I didn't know, but whose blood has saved my life.
And I think it's that time or later, ask for my father for prayer what to say.
But anyway, the next morning they had gone.
And I took another jacket-- because it was mostly the jacket which was bloodied-- another jacket out of my suitcase, and then went back to work.
Now, next comes when I-- we had to plan for escape.
